metadata.summary: Developer-facing API docs with Quick Start, auth examples, OpenAPI endpoints with multiple examples, error codes, SDKs, and changelog kept in sync via test validation.

content.title: API Documentation
content.summary: Developers abandon APIs with poor documentation — missing authentication examples and absent error tables are the top complaints. OpenAPI's examples field lets you embed multiple named request variants directly in the spec; Swagger UI renders them as selectable try-it-out presets. Keeping examples verified by tests prevents docs from drifting from the actual API behaviour.

why.p: Developers scan docs in a predictable order: quick start first to verify the API works, auth second to unblock, then endpoints. Missing any section causes abandonment at that step. Spec-as-source (OpenAPI) with multiple rendered views (Swagger UI for try-it-out, Redoc for reading, Stoplight for team design) prevents docs from drifting. Examples embedded in the OpenAPI spec and validated by tests stay in sync with reality; hand-written docs inevitably diverge.

list.item: Launching a new API to external or internal consumers
Migrating from ad-hoc Markdown docs to an OpenAPI-driven setup
When support tickets repeatedly ask for authentication examples or error code meanings
Generating client SDKs from the spec
Purely internal service-to-service APIs where code is the contract and no SDK is needed
Prototype APIs under active design flux — write docs after the contract stabilises
Micro-service mesh where service mesh observability tools (Envoy, Istio) already surface contracts

section.name: doc-structure
section.p: Every external API must document: Overview (one paragraph), Quick Start (working curl commands), Authentication (token format, headers, flows), Endpoints (grouped by resource), Error Codes (table with code, meaning, solution), and Changelog. Developers scan docs in this order: quick start first to verify the API works, auth second to unblock, then endpoints. Missing any section causes abandonment at that step. For multi-language client SDK distributions, docs must include curl + JS + Python + Go snippets that stay in sync. Hosted developer portals should surface auth flow, rate limits, plan tiers, status page, and a "Try it" sandbox to reduce time-to-first-call. LLM-driven documentation runs leverage agents excelling at templating per-endpoint pages from spec metadata, though human review on prose and security guidance remains critical.

rule.statement: Every external API must document: Overview (one paragraph), Quick Start (working curl commands), Authentication (token format, headers, flows), Endpoints (grouped by resource), Error Codes (table with code, meaning, solution), SDKs (install commands and basic usage per language), and Changelog (version history with breaking change markers).
rule.rationale: Developers scan docs in this order: quick start first to verify the API works, auth second to unblock, then endpoints. Missing any section causes abandonment at that step.

list.item: Overview — what the API does and who it is for
Quick Start — working curl commands a developer can copy and run in under 5 minutes
Authentication — token acquisition, header format, refresh flow
Endpoints — grouped by resource with request/response examples
Error Codes — table: code | meaning | solution
SDKs — install commands and basic usage per language
Changelog — version history with breaking change markers

section.name: quick-start-pattern
section.p: The Quick Start section must contain at most three commands: one to get credentials, one to make a first request, one to verify the response. Commands must be copy-pasteable with no modifications. Every placeholder ({YOUR_KEY}, [INSERT_VALUE]) in a Quick Start is a drop-off point. Real working examples with disposable test credentials or clear substitution instructions reduce time-to-first-call.

rule.statement: The Quick Start section must contain at most three commands: one to get credentials, one to make a first request, one to verify the response. Commands must be copy-pasteable with no modifications.
rule.rationale: Every placeholder ({YOUR_KEY}, [INSERT_VALUE]) in a Quick Start is a drop-off point. Real working examples with disposable test credentials or clear substitution instructions reduce time-to-first-call.

example.code:
# 1. Get API key curl -X POST https://api.example.com/auth/register \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"email": "you@example.com"}'
# 2. Make first request curl -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_KEY" \ https://api.example.com/users

section.name: error-code-table
section.p: Document every non-200 status code the API can return, including the error code string, HTTP status, description, and a concrete solution the developer can take. Error tables are the most-searched section of API docs. Without them, developers file support tickets for errors they could resolve themselves.

rule.statement: Document every non-200 status code the API can return, including the error code string, HTTP status, description, and a concrete solution the developer can take.
rule.rationale: Error tables are the most-searched section of API docs. Without them, developers file support tickets for errors they could resolve themselves.

example.note: Error table format with actionable solution column.
example.code: | Code | Status | Meaning | Solution | |------|--------|---------|----------| | validation_error | 400 | Invalid request body | Check required fields and formats | | unauthorized | 401 | Missing or invalid API key | Verify Authorization header | | rate_limited | 429 | Too many requests | Wait Retry-After seconds | | internal_error | 500 | Server error | Retry with exponential backoff |

section.name: changelog
section.p: Maintain a changelog with version, date, and a bulleted list of changes. Mark breaking changes explicitly. Keep at least the last 5 versions. API consumers need to know when to update their integrations. Unmarked breaking changes cause production incidents.

rule.statement: Maintain a changelog with version, date, and a bulleted list of changes. Mark breaking changes explicitly. Keep at least the last 5 versions.
rule.rationale: API consumers need to know when to update their integrations. Unmarked breaking changes cause production incidents.

section.name: openapi-examples
section.p: OpenAPI examples field usage for named request variants, FastAPI app metadata setup, and multi-tool documentation strategy. Use the examples field (not example) on requestBody content to provide multiple named variants. Each variant should cover a distinct use case, edge case, or role-based input. Named examples appear as selectable presets in Swagger UI, letting developers explore the API's range without reading prose. Multiple examples expose edge cases (e.g. admin vs basic user) that a single example hides.

rule.statement: Use the examples field (not example) on requestBody content to provide multiple named variants. Each variant should cover a distinct use case, edge case, or role-based input.
rule.rationale: Named examples appear as selectable presets in Swagger UI, letting developers explore the API's range without reading prose. Multiple examples expose edge cases (e.g. admin vs basic user) that a single example hides.

example.code: paths: /users: post: summary: Create user requestBody: content: application/json: schema: $ref: '#/components/schemas/CreateUser' examples: basic: summary: Basic user value: name: "John Doe" email: "john@example.com" with-role: summary: User with admin role value: name: "Admin User" email: "admin@example.com" role: "admin"

antipattern.description: A single example covering only the happy path with no edge cases.
antipattern.reason: Developers copy the example verbatim; untested inputs (special characters, optional fields, boundary values) cause production errors.

section.name: fastapi-metadata
section.p: Configure the FastAPI app with title, description (markdown with auth and rate limit info), version, docs_url, and redoc_url. The description field should contain the authentication header format and rate limit tiers. FastAPI generates OpenAPI spec from the app metadata; everything in description appears in Swagger UI and Redoc without any separate documentation maintenance.

rule.statement: Configure the FastAPI app with title, description (markdown with auth and rate limit info), version, docs_url, and redoc_url. The description field should contain the authentication header format and rate limit tiers.
rule.rationale: FastAPI generates OpenAPI spec from the app metadata; everything in description appears in Swagger UI and Redoc without any separate documentation maintenance.

section.name: documentation-tools
section.p: For interactive try-it-out: use Swagger UI. For clean readable reference docs: use Redoc. For team API design: use Stoplight Elements. Do not maintain separate Markdown docs that duplicate the OpenAPI spec. Duplicate documentation drifts. The OpenAPI spec is the single source of truth; rendering tools derive human-readable output from it automatically.

rule.statement: For interactive try-it-out: use Swagger UI. For clean readable reference docs: use Redoc. For team API design: use Stoplight Elements. Do not maintain separate Markdown docs that duplicate the OpenAPI spec.
rule.rationale: Duplicate documentation drifts. The OpenAPI spec is the single source of truth; rendering tools derive human-readable output from it automatically.

list.item: Swagger UI — interactive, try-it-out, auto-generated from FastAPI
Redoc — clean multi-panel reference, better for reading than testing
Stoplight — visual API design + linting (use for design-first workflow)
Postman — team collaboration, collection sharing, test runner

section.name: known-failures
section.p: Several failure modes commonly encountered when adopting API documentation practices: Spec-doc drift occurs when docs lag the spec because nobody regenerates; mitigation involves CI generating openapi.yaml from code or running Spectral lint plus redocly preview-docs. Examples rot when hand-edited curl snippets reference old field names; tests must verify request/response examples against the schema using schemathesis or dredd. Auth flow vagueness happens when "Use your API key" lacks step-by-step instructions; always document the full obtain → store → send → rotate flow with code snippets. Rate-limit info gets hidden, leaving developers surprised by 429 responses; docs must surface limits per plan, headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After), and recommended backoff. Search breaks when themes ship with weak search; Algolia DocSearch or Meilisearch restore function. Versioning becomes unclear when /v1 docs URL silently switches to v2 content; pin a /v1/ path and add banners linking to /v2/. No changelog causes silent breaking changes; maintain CHANGELOG.md rendered at /changelog with semver tags. PII and internal hostnames leak into examples; use deterministic fakers and gate with grep CI. Internationalization goes half-done with translated landing but English-only reference; pick English-only (most common) or full localization, never in-between. Try-it sandbox hits production when triggering real charges; provide a sandbox env (api-sandbox.example.com) with pre-auth test keys.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive doc work spec-first. A spec-curator subagent ensures every operation has summary, description, operationId, tags, request/response examples. A prose-author subagent writes concept pages (auth, pagination, errors, rate limits, webhooks) using shared partials reusable in renderers. A codegen-snippets subagent produces curl + Python + JS + Go examples per endpoint, sourcing values from spec examples. A link-and-anchor checker verifies internal links and that every error code mentioned in prose exists in the registry. The faion-sdd-executor-agent runs lint + build + preview deploy. Agents must never edit prose without human review pass — reference and conceptual docs are the public face of the API. Recommended tools include redocly for OpenAPI lint and render (https://redocly.com/docs/cli), spectral for lint OpenAPI completeness (https://stoplight.io/open-source/spectral), mintlify for doc framework with OpenAPI ingest (https://mintlify.com/docs), swagger-ui for try-it-out reference (https://swagger.io/tools/swagger-ui/), redoc-cli for standalone rendering (https://github.com/Redocly/redoc), widdershins for OpenAPI to Markdown conversion (https://github.com/Mermade/widdershins), lychee for fast link checking (https://github.com/lycheeverse/lychee), and algolia docsearch for hosted docs search (https://docsearch.algolia.com). Services include Mintlify (SaaS, git-driven, OpenAPI ingest, AI search built-in), Readme.io (SaaS, hosted portal with Try-it), Stoplight (SaaS/OSS, visual API design + hosted docs), Bump.sh (SaaS, spec hosting + breaking-change diffing), Redocly (SaaS/OSS, paid platform with versioning), Postman Public Docs (SaaS, auto-generated from collections), GitBook (SaaS, markdown-first), and Algolia DocSearch (SaaS free for OSS).

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Common pitfalls when agents generate API documentation: Hallucinated endpoints/fields occur when agent invents /users/:id/profile from "intuition"; always render from spec, reject prose endpoint references not in the spec. Drift between snippet and schema happens when agent updates schema but not the curl example; validate examples against schema in CI. Pasting real tokens/customer IDs bleeds secrets; pre-commit grep for token regex. Vague auth prose says "Use your API key" with no example; force prompt to require obtain → store → send sequence. Inconsistent terminology uses "user", "account", "member" interchangeably; maintain glossary and lint for synonyms. Localization mid-page leaves half-translated pages worse than English-only; pick policy. Try-it defaults pointing at prod is dangerous; default to sandbox, require explicit env switch. Missing breaking-change callouts when renamed enum or removed field occurs; oasdiff must run in CI. Outdated rate-limit numbers happen when pricing changes, docs lag; source numbers from single config file referenced in docs. Heavy screenshots without alt text fail accessibility; lychee --accept ignores image alts, mandate alt text. Code blocks without language tag lose syntax highlight and SEO; lint for fenced blocks. Webhooks documented but no signature verification snippet means integrators skip security; provide working code per language.

template.purpose: FastAPI app with title, description, version, auth docs, Swagger/Redoc URLs
OpenAPI path item with multiple named request/response examples
